**

# MAAI Blog Production SOP

Owner: Kriti · Last updated: {update on edit} · Applies to: all client blog content before it goes live

  [[First Claude Response]]

This document is the single quality gate for every blog post MAAI publishes, on any client site. Part 1 is the gate. Part 2 covers how images are sourced and how they earn backlinks at the same time.

  

Two rules sit above everything else:

  

1. We optimise existing pages before we write new ones. A new post is only justified when there is a real keyword opportunity that an existing page cannot serve.
    
2. Every post targets a buyer, a user, or a warm lead. We are not chasing pure traffic.
    

  

---

## Part 1 — Blog Review Checklist (the gate)

Phase 1 runs once a month and sets the topic list. Phase 0 and Phases 2 through 8 run for every individual post. A post goes live only when Phase 0 and every box in Phases 2 through 8 are checked. If anything fails, send it back with notes. Do not partially publish.

### Phase 0 — Keyword and intent (per post, before a single word is written)

- ![unticked](data:image/png;base64,)
    
    The target keyword comes from a real opportunity, not a guess. Source it from one of these:
    

- Google Search Console queries where the page sits in position 3 to 20 with existing impressions.
    
- Commercial-intent terms (best, top, versus, review, comparison, alternative) the site already surfaces for.
    
- A bottom-of-funnel modifier pattern: [bottom-of-funnel keyword] for [plural audience], for example "expense tracking software for clinics". These look like zero-volume terms in standard tools but convert and stack.
    

- ![unticked](data:image/png;base64,)
    
    The intent is bottom or middle of funnel. If it is pure top-of-funnel traffic with no path to a customer, it does not get written.
    
- ![unticked](data:image/png;base64,)
    
    We have confirmed an existing page cannot be optimised to win this instead. If one can, we improve that page rather than adding a new URL.
    
- ![unticked](data:image/png;base64,)
    
    The keyword is logged in the client content calendar with its GSC position and impressions at time of selection.
    

### Phase 1 — Research and topic selection (monthly)

Run once at the start of each month. This phase fires before any per-post work. Output is a locked list of topics for the month, mapped to clients and intent.

  

Part A: Research (SEMRUSH, GSC, Reddit)

  

- ![unticked|72](data:image/png;base64,)
    
    SEMRUSH: Continuously pull new content opportunities per client. Capture search volume and keyword difficulty (KD) for each idea so we go after winnable terms, not vanity ones.
    
- ![unticked](data:image/png;base64,)
    
    Public questions: Mine what the audience is actually asking. Reddit and Quora via site:reddit.com OR site:quora.com [client niche] "how do I" OR "why does", plus People Also Ask and Answer The Public. These become real H2s and FAQ sections.
    
- ![unticked](data:image/png;base64,)
    
    EEAT fit: Prioritise topics where the client can show genuine Experience, Expertise, Authoritativeness, and Trust. A topic only makes the shortlist if the client has a real basis to be credible on it.
    
- ![unticked](data:image/png;base64,)
    
    GSC improvement layer: Pull keywords already performing on Google Search Console (position 3 to 20 with impressions) that an existing or new piece can push higher.
    

  

Part B: Decide blog topics for the month

  

- ![unticked](data:image/png;base64,)
    
    Consolidate Part A into a single shortlist.
    
- ![unticked](data:image/png;base64,)
    
    Tag each topic with its client and its funnel stage (bottom or mid only).
    
- ![unticked](data:image/png;base64,)
    
    Flag which are "new post" versus "improve existing" so we do not write a new URL when an existing page can win.
    
- ![unticked](data:image/png;base64,)
    
    Lock the month's topics into the client content calendar (Mahima's calendar), with the source data (volume, KD, GSC position) attached to each.
    

### Phase 2 — Structure and SEO foundation

- ![unticked](data:image/png;base64,)
    
    H1 is keyword rich and reads naturally to the audience.
    
- ![unticked](data:image/png;base64,)
    
    H2 outline fully satisfies the promise of the H1. Each H2 earns its place.
    
- ![unticked](data:image/png;base64,)
    
    The exact target keyword appears high on the page, ideally in the first paragraph.
    
- ![unticked](data:image/png;base64,)
    
    A TLDR of two to three sentences sits at the very top, giving the main points immediately. This is non-negotiable. It lifts conversions and gives AI search a clean answer to lift.
    
- ![unticked](data:image/png;base64,)
    
    Page title, meta description, and URL slug are written. The meta description is confident and direct.
    
- ![unticked](data:image/png;base64,)
    
    Heading hierarchy is clean: one H1, logical H2s, H3s only where they genuinely nest.
    

### Phase 3 — Brand voice and language (MAAI house style)

These rules are absolute. They apply to every word, on every client.

  

- ![unticked](data:image/png;base64,)
    
    No em dashes. Anywhere.
    
- ![unticked](data:image/png;base64,)
    
    No AI filler words. Strike "streamlined", "seamless", "leverage", "unlock", "robust", "elevate", "navigate the landscape", "in today's fast-paced world", and similar.
    
- ![unticked](data:image/png;base64,)
    
    No "it is not X, it is Y" construction. Rewrite the sentence properly.
    
- ![unticked](data:image/png;base64,)
    
    The copy speaks directly to the reader in the client's tone. Second person where it fits.
    
- ![unticked](data:image/png;base64,)
    
    No racist, colonial, or culturally misplaced language. No words or framings that assume a default culture or carry that baggage.
    
- ![unticked](data:image/png;base64,)
    
    No negative framing of any competitor. We describe what the client does well. Comparison pages use neutral, factual language about other options. We never put another brand down.
    
- ![unticked](data:image/png;base64,)
    
    Paragraphs are short and easily readable. No walls of text.
    
- ![unticked](data:image/png;base64,)
    
    The writing sounds like a knowledgeable person talking to a smart friend. Professional, clear, natural. It does not read like a press release.
    

### Phase 4 — Accuracy and hallucination check

AI-assisted drafts carry invented facts. This phase exists to catch them.

  

- ![unticked](data:image/png;base64,)
    
    A human has read the full post line by line. Not skimmed.
    
- ![unticked](data:image/png;base64,)
    
    Every statistic, figure, and claim is traced to a real, current source. Anything that cannot be verified is removed, not softened.
    
- ![unticked](data:image/png;base64,)
    
    No invented quotes or attributions. If a quote is used, it is real and correctly credited.
    
- ![unticked](data:image/png;base64,)
    
    No misarticulated ideas, no contradictions, no sentences that collapse under a second read.
    
- ![unticked](data:image/png;base64,)
    
    If the post uses substantial AI-written sections, it carries a visible line that it is edited and reviewed by a human. (This is the format the largest SEO publishers now use, and it keeps us on the right side of Google's scaled-content rules.)
    

### Phase 5 — Media

- ![unticked](data:image/png;base64,)
    
    At least one original or genuinely relevant image is present. Real photos, location shots, data visualisations, infographics, or charts. (Sourcing is covered in Part 2.)
    
- ![unticked](data:image/png;base64,)
    
    Where the client has worked with recognisable companies, their logos appear as social proof, with permission.
    
- ![unticked](data:image/png;base64,)
    
    Video is embedded where it adds value.
    
- ![unticked](data:image/png;base64,)
    
    Every image has descriptive alt text. The target keyword appears in alt text where it reads naturally, never forced.
    
- ![unticked](data:image/png;base64,)
    
    Images are compressed and correctly sized. Page weight is checked.
    

### Phase 6 — Conversion

A ranking post that does not convert has done half a job.

  

- ![unticked](data:image/png;base64,)
    
    There is a clear call to action to use the client's product or service.
    
- ![unticked](data:image/png;base64,)
    
    The path forward is obvious from the page. The reader knows the next step.
    
- ![unticked](data:image/png;base64,)
    
    Internal links point the reader toward the client's product, service, or a related useful page.
    
- ![unticked](data:image/png;base64,)
    
    The page reads as built for this specific reader. Where a keyword is highly valuable, it gets its own dedicated section or page so the reader feels "this is for me".
    

### Phase 7 — Interlinking and indexing

- ![unticked](data:image/png;base64,)
    
    The new post is interlinked from 2 to 3 existing pages that already rank, using keyword-rich anchor text.
    
- ![unticked](data:image/png;base64,)
    
    The post sits in a sensible place in the site architecture. Important pages stay close to the homepage so they carry more weight.
    
- ![unticked](data:image/png;base64,)
    
    The URL is submitted in Google Search Console and Request Indexing is clicked.
    
- ![unticked](data:image/png;base64,)
    
    The sitemap includes the new URL.
    

### Phase 8 — AI search readiness

- ![unticked](data:image/png;base64,)
    
    The post answers the way people actually phrase things to AI. Long, natural-language, seven-words-or-more questions get H2 sections or their own answers.
    
- ![unticked](data:image/png;base64,)
    
    Where we know the query fan-out language an AI uses for this topic (pulled from the ChatGPT or Perplexity inspect method), that language appears in H2s and body copy.
    
- ![unticked](data:image/png;base64,)
    
    The TLDR gives AI a clean, liftable summary near the top.
    

---

## Part 2 — Blog Image Sourcing Workflow

This workflow sources images for the post and turns them into a backlink engine at the same time. Run it for every post that needs original media.

### Step 1 — Define the image need

For each post, list what is actually required:

  

- One hero image.
    
- In-body images that break up the text and illustrate real points.
    
- At least one original asset where possible: a data visualisation, an infographic, a chart, or a genuine photo.
    

### Step 2 — Source in priority order

1. Original photos. Shot on a phone, relevant to the client's niche or location. These are the most defensible and the most useful for backlinks later.
    
2. Created assets in Canva. Built to the client's brand, not MAAI's. (For MAAI's own content, use the MAAI system: Playfair Display, DM Mono, Bebas Neue, dark background, crimson accents.) Niche-specific graphics, data visualisations, infographics, charts.
    
3. Free stock only as a last resort and never as the primary image. Original always wins for both ranking and link building.
    

### Step 3 — The backlink play (run on original images only)

Original images double as a passive backlink source. Set this up once per batch of originals:

  

- ![unticked](data:image/png;base64,)
    
    Upload the original images to free stock platforms with high domain authority: Unsplash, Pexels, Pixabay, Flickr Creative Commons.
    
- ![unticked](data:image/png;base64,)
    
    In each platform's attribution or credit field, link back to the client's website. When bloggers, journalists, and designers use the image, that credit becomes a free backlink.
    
- ![unticked](data:image/png;base64,)
    
    Monthly, reverse image search the originals on Google Images and TinEye to find sites already using them without proper attribution.
    
- ![unticked](data:image/png;base64,)
    
    Email those site owners and ask for a source credit link. Conversion is high because they already used the work and know they owe the credit. This can be templated or handed to an assistant.
    

  

This compounds. The more original images we put out, the more links the client earns with no further outreach.

### Step 4 — On-page image hygiene

Before the image goes into the post:

  

- ![unticked](data:image/png;base64,)
    
    Compressed for web. Page speed protected.
    
- ![unticked](data:image/png;base64,)
    
    Correctly dimensioned. Upscale only when needed (Pillow / LANCZOS), never stretch.
    
- ![unticked](data:image/png;base64,)
    
    File named with the keyword, words separated by hyphens.
    
- ![unticked](data:image/png;base64,)
    
    Descriptive alt text added (see Phase 5).
    

### Image tools reference

|Need|Tool|
|---|---|
|Created graphics, infographics, charts|Canva|

  

---

## Quick reference: the 60-second pre-publish scan

For a post that has already been through the full gate, this is the final glance before hitting publish:

  

1. TLDR at the top?
    
2. Keyword high on the page and in the H1?
    
3. No em dashes, no filler words, no competitor put-downs?
    
4. Every stat verified?
    
5. Original image present, alt text done?
    
6. Clear CTA?
    
7. Interlinked from 2 to 3 ranking pages?
    
8. Submitted to GSC, indexing requested?
    

  

If all eight are yes, publish.

  
**
